payload.input: Downstairs, their parents move through the kitchen like ghosts performing a familiar haunt. Her father’s hands tremble as he buttons his shirt, the memory of his rage still hanging in the air like smoke. He has not touched the belt since Sunday, has not raised his voice above a whisper. Her mother packs their Bible study bags with methodical precision, her lips moving in silent prayer, her eyes flicking toward the stairs every few seconds to ensure her children have not evaporated into the corruption she still fears. "You’ll stay inside," her father says at the door, his keys jangling in his fist, his gaze fixed on the purity ring on Maya’s hand as if it might protect her from herself. "Lock the deadbolts. Do not answer the door unless it is Pastor Jim. We will be back by noon."  Maya nods, her face arranged into the mask of the traumatized innocent, the girl who was falsely accused and must now be protected from the world’s digital filth. "I’ll read Proverbs," she promises, her voice small and sincere. "Chapter five. About the adulteress."  Her mother’s face crumples with grateful tears. She pulls Maya into a hug that smells of lavender and anxiety, her hands patting Maya’s back with a desperation that says she is trying to press the goodness back into her daughter’s skin through force of will. When the car pulls out of the driveway, gravel crunching beneath the tires, Maya counts to sixty, then to one hundred and twenty, ensuring they have turned the corner before she moves. Ethan is already at the window, peering through the blinds with his body rigid as a hunting dog scenting quarry. "They said they’d come at nine-thirty," he whispers, his voice cracking on the time. "It’s nine-fifteen."  Maya retrieves the phone from beneath her pillow, checking the messages. A new one from ScholarlyPursuits, timestamped 8:47 AM: "En route. Black Volvo parked three houses down since dawn. Julian is wearing a wire for our preliminary documentation.
